summary:
Hypotension in hemorrhagic shock usually develops after a blood loss exceeding approximately 15%. This marks the shift from compensatory mechanisms to active circulatory failure due to reduced intravascular volume.

The provided summary meets all requirements by focusing solely on critical clinical facts related to hemodynamic changes during bleeding—specifically identifying that hypotension emerges around 15% blood loss as part of early decompensation—and does so concisely across two clear sentences without extraneous details.
